metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in What/files item 1 whether loss detection should inspect the STATUS event's originating clone UUID (consistent with the lower-UUID-wins rule described in Why) rather than the assignee field, and reconcile the parenthetical with the Why section's description of the two independent resolution keys.
findings.finding: The post-claim detection logic (item 1) equates 'winning STATUS event is not this clone's' with 'assignee ≠ this claimant', but the Why section explicitly states STATUS and assignee are resolved by *different* rules (lower-UUID-wins vs. last-writer-wins by HLC). These can produce different winners, so using assignee as the STATUS-loss proxy is unreliable and contradicts the problem description.
findings.location: Why section (STATUS vs assignee resolution rules) vs. What/files item 1 ('assignee ≠ this claimant, i.e. our claim lost the fork')
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'assignee ≠ this claimant' with 'the STATUS event whose uuid is lowest (the surviving event after fork resolution) was not emitted by this clone' — i.e. compare the event's clone/origin ID, not the assignee value, to determine who won the STATUS fork.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge and mitigate the new reducer→alert_store coupling in the plan: specify that the `alert_store.append` call must be idempotent (e.g., keyed on the dropped event UUID to prevent duplicate writes on replay), and capture this architectural decision — that the reducer gains a durable side-effect — in an ADR or CLAUDE.md update rather than only in `docs/concurrency.md`.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new cross-component dependency — `reducer/_processors.py` writing a durable marker via `alert_store.append` — without acknowledging, justifying, or mitigating the coupling. Reducers are typically stateless/pure replay components; adding a side-effecting `alert_store.append` call inside fork-resolution logic risks duplicate alert writes on every replay of the same event sequence. No interface or event boundary is proposed, and no ADR or design-doc update captures this architectural decision.
findings.location: What / files — item 2: reducer/_processors.py ~L159-167 + alert_store.append / bridge_alerts dir; also AC line 2
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note in the plan: the marker write in `_processors.py` must be guarded by a check (e.g., `if not alert_store.exists(dropped_uuid)`) to be idempotent under replay. Add an ADR entry (or CLAUDE.md section) documenting the decision to allow reducers to emit durable alerts as a side-effect during fork resolution, and why a separate post-replay notification step was not chosen instead.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify how `alert_store.append(record, repo_root)` is called from `reducer/_processors.py` — specify either (a) how `repo_root` is threaded through the reducer's call chain, or (b) adopt an alternative durable-marker location (e.g. emit a side-channel return value from `reduce_ticket` that the caller in `fsck`/`claim` writes to the alert store with its own `repo_root`).
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'reuse `alert_store.append`, `bridge_alerts` dir' from within `reducer/_processors.py`, but `alert_store` is located in `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/alert_store.py` — a different subsystem — and its `append(record, repo_root: Path)` signature requires a `repo_root` argument that the pure-replay reducer processor functions do not receive. No `repo_root` is threaded through any processor signature in `_processors.py`. The plan does not address how `repo_root` reaches the reducer or how the cross-subsystem import is justified.
findings.location: What/files §2 — 'Durable surfacing — reducer/_processors.py ~L159-167, record a durable marker (reuse `alert_store.append`, `bridge_alerts` dir)'
findings.suggested_fix: Move the durable-marker write OUT of the pure reducer and into the call sites that already have `repo_root` (e.g. `claim_compute` after merge, or a dedicated post-replay hook in `fsck`), passing a callback or checking a flag in the returned state. This preserves the reducer's stateless-function contract and avoids a reconciler → reducer layering violation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `alert_store.append` is reached from `reducer/_processors.py`: either thread `repo_root` through `process_status` (and all callers of `reduce_ticket`), inject an optional callback, or move the durable-marker write to a post-reduce hook in `claim_compute` (where `repo_root` is already available) — and name the chosen mechanism in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan directs `reducer/_processors.py` to call `alert_store.append` (from `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/alert_store.py`), but `_processors.py` currently has zero imports from `rebar._engine`, and `alert_store` requires a `repo_root: Path` argument that `process_status` does not receive. The import path and argument-threading mechanism are entirely unspecified, making this a hallucinated edit target for the durable-marker work item.
findings.location: What/files §2 — 'Durable surfacing' / reducer/_processors.py ~L159-167
findings.suggested_fix: Move the durable-marker write out of `_processors.py` entirely. After `reduce_ticket` returns in the post-claim verification step (§1, `claim_compute`), the caller already has `repo_root` and the reduced state; write the fork marker there (or in a new `_commands/claim.py` helper) rather than inside the pure reducer.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `STATUS_FORK_RESOLVED` (lowercased: `status_fork_resolved`) to `_STRUCTURED_KINDS` in `fsck.py` so the JSON transform extracts `ticket_id` and `detail` correctly for the new finding.
findings.finding: `_STRUCTURED_KINDS` in `fsck.py` (L34-41) must include the new `STATUS_FORK_RESOLVED` tag for `_transform_json` to extract `ticket_id` and `detail` into structured JSON output; the plan does not mention this required additive change.
findings.location: What/files §3 — 'fsck/validate/show' / src/rebar/_commands/fsck.py
findings.suggested_fix: Add `"status_fork_resolved"` to the `_STRUCTURED_KINDS` frozenset at fsck.py:L34-41.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a documentation step to §What/files (e.g. §5) that names `docs/concurrency.md` as an edit target and describes what to add (e.g. a subsection on cross-clone claim fork detection, the exit-10 retroactive path, and the fsck/show surfacing behaviour).
findings.finding: `docs/concurrency.md` (required by AC4) is not listed in the Scope 'In' list or §What/files, leaving AC4 untracked by any implementation step.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4 ('docs/concurrency.md notes the surfacing behaviour') / Scope section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that post-claim fork-loss detection requires a prior fetch/sync to bring the winning clone's events into the local working tree; specify WHERE (e.g., at next read-sync / `show` / `list` time, after the merge is actually visible locally) the exit-10 or equivalent signal fires — not immediately after `push_after_commit`.
findings.finding: The post-claim verification in claim_compute (step 1) cannot detect a cross-clone fork loss at claim time: push_after_commit is best-effort and never raises, and even a successful push only updates origin — the local working tree still contains only this clone's events. A reduce_ticket call immediately after push_after_commit reads the same pre-merge local state as before the push. The other clone's STATUS event becomes locally visible only on the NEXT read-side sync (fetch+merge), not at claim_compute time. Consequently the proposed 'retroactive exit-10 path' cannot fire at the point the plan specifies.
findings.location: What / files — item 1: 'claim_compute (~L170-175, after push.push_after_commit): re-read the ticket from the merged store'
findings.suggested_fix: Move the fork-loss detection to the read-sync path: after a fetch+merge makes the other clone's STATUS events visible, the reducer's process_status already fires PARENT_CHAIN_FORK_RESOLVED. A thin post-reduce check in show/list (compare state['env_id'] or the winning STATUS event's env_id to the local env_id) can surface the loss. The durable marker written during reducer fork resolution (step 2) provides the necessary information at that point.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the alert_store.append approach with the codebase-native BRIDGE_ALERT event mechanism: write a STATUS_FORK_RESOLVED BRIDGE_ALERT event file (one-time, under the write lock, not in the reducer) when a fork is resolved, so it appears in state.bridge_alerts and is surfaced by the existing fsck/show paths without cross-layer coupling or repeated firing.
findings.finding: Step 2 proposes calling 'alert_store.append, bridge_alerts dir' from reducer/_processors.py when a STATUS fork is resolved, but alert_store lives in the Jira reconciler tier (src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/alert_store.py) and writes to bridge_state/bridge_alerts/ — a reconciler-specific path. Calling it from the pure replay reducer creates a cross-layer coupling, writes to the wrong path for non-Jira users, and fires on every reduce_ticket call after a merge (non-idempotent). The plan gives no positive rationale for this choice over the existing codebase-native mechanism (writing a BRIDGE_ALERT event, which process_bridge_alert already handles and state.bridge_alerts already surfaces to fsck/show).
findings.location: What / files — item 2: 'record a durable marker (reuse alert_store.append, bridge_alerts dir)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `alert_store.append` (requiring `repo_root: Path`) is reachable from `reducer/_processors.py`'s `process_status` call-chain, or replace with an alternative that doesn't cross the reducer↔reconciler package boundary (e.g., store a `fork_resolved` marker in compiled ticket state for fsck/show to consume).
findings.finding: The plan instructs `reducer/_processors.py` to call `alert_store.append` (from `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/alert_store.py`) during fork resolution, but this is an unverified capability: (a) the reducer package has no current imports from the reconciler-internal package; (b) `alert_store.append` requires a `repo_root: Path` argument that is not available in the `process_status(state, event, data, filepath)` signature; and (c) the reducer is stateless and has no established mechanism for side-effecting an I/O store during replay — coupling replay to I/O is architecturally novel and unjustified vs. a cheaper alternative (e.g., recording a fork-resolution flag in the ticket's compiled state for fsck/show to read).
findings.location: What/files §2 — 'Durable surfacing — reducer/_processors.py ~L159-167, record a durable marker (reuse alert_store.append, bridge_alerts dir)'
findings.suggested_fix: Instead of calling alert_store.append inside the reducer, add a `fork_resolved` key to the compiled state dict when a STATUS fork is resolved (the reducer already owns state mutation). fsck and show can then read this directly from compiled state — no cross-package I/O, no repo_root threading, and full compatibility with the reducer's stateless-replay contract.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `status_fork_resolved` (lowercased) must be added to `_STRUCTURED_KINDS` in `fsck.py` so the `--output json` transform extracts `ticket_id` and `detail` correctly for the new finding.
findings.finding: The new fsck tag `STATUS_FORK_RESOLVED` is not registered in `_STRUCTURED_KINDS`, leaving its JSON output shape undefined.
findings.location: What/files §3 (fsck.py finding); src/rebar/_commands/fsck.py:34-40 (_STRUCTURED_KINDS)
findings.suggested_fix: Add `"status_fork_resolved"` to the `_STRUCTURED_KINDS` set in `fsck.py:34-40`, and assert in the regression test that the JSON output contains `{kind: 'status_fork_resolved', ticket_id: '<id>', detail: '...'}` (not just the text line).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete marker-write call-site and mechanism for the fork-resolved durable record: either (a) place the write in a post-replay hook outside the reducer (e.g., in `_api.py` or `claim_compute` after merge), passing `repo_root`, or (b) define a reducer-layer marker format (e.g., a `.fork_resolved` dot-file per ticket) that fsck can read without depending on `alert_store`, and update Item 3 accordingly.
findings.finding: The plan directs calling `alert_store.append` from inside `reducer/_processors.py::process_status` (the durable marker, Item 2), but the reducer is a layering-constrained pure-replay module with no access to `repo_root: Path` and is explicitly prohibited from importing engine-layer modules.
findings.location: What/files §2 (reducer/_processors.py ~L159-167); src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py:250 (layer boundary comment); src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/alert_store.py:40 (append signature)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify the import/call mechanism for `alert_store.append` from `reducer/_processors.py`: specify whether a direct import from `rebar._engine.rebar_reconciler.alert_store` is permitted (and document the new dependency arc), or whether an alternative signal path (e.g., returning fork metadata to the caller for the caller to persist) should be used instead — and confirm that the chosen approach has ≥1 existing precedent in the reducer layer.
findings.finding: The plan directs `reducer/_processors.py` to call `alert_store.append` (Step 2: 'reuse `alert_store.append`, `bridge_alerts` dir'), but `alert_store` lives in `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/` and is currently loaded only via a dynamic namespace loader (`rebar_reconciler.alert_store`) within the rebar_reconciler subsystem. The reducer package has zero existing calls to alert_store (grep confirms no hits in `src/rebar/reducer/`). Importing it from `_processors.py` either (a) introduces a new direct import dependency arc from `reducer` → `_engine/rebar_reconciler` that doesn't currently exist, or (b) requires replicating the non-trivial `_load('rebar_reconciler.alert_store', 'alert_store.py')` dynamic-loader pattern used in `reconcile.py`. Neither path is described or justified in the plan, making this a premature cross-layer coupling with 0 existing call-sites from the reducer layer.
findings.location: What/files §2 — 'Durable surfacing' (`reducer/_processors.py` ~L159-167, `alert_store.append`)
findings.suggested_fix: Consider having `process_status` return fork metadata (e.g., a `ForkResolved` namedtuple) instead of writing the durable marker directly; let the caller in the reducer's reduce loop or the claim path write the alert_store entry via the already-established _load() plumbing in reconcile.py or claim.py. This keeps the reducer layer side-effect-free and avoids the new dependency arc.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how repo_root reaches process_status for the alert_store.append call — either thread it through replay_events/_ReplayCtx/process_status, or adopt an alternative persistence mechanism (e.g. have the reducer return side-effect records for the caller to persist, or write the marker from the claim.py post-verification step instead of the replay layer).
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'reuse alert_store.append' from inside reducer/_processors.py process_status, but alert_store.append requires a repo_root: Path argument that is not available anywhere in the reducer's call stack — process_status receives only (state, event, data, filepath) and the replay layer is architecturally isolated from the reconciler-tier store. No mechanism for obtaining or threading repo_root into process_status is described.
findings.location: What / files — item 2: 'when the reducer resolves a STATUS fork (reducer/_processors.py ~L159-167), record a durable marker (reuse alert_store.append, bridge_alerts dir)'
findings.suggested_fix: Move the durable marker write to the post-claim verification step in claim.py (item 1), where repo_root is already available. After re-reading the merged store, if the fork is detected (winning assignee ≠ this clone's claim), call alert_store.append from claim.py rather than from process_status. For the fsck surface, write the marker file directly from claim.py on fork-detection, not from the reducer. This avoids threading repo_root into the pure replay layer entirely.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the durable marker write is idempotent under replay (e.g., use a content-addressed key like `{ticket_id}:{dropped_uuid}` so re-appending is a no-op, or check for existence before writing).
findings.finding: Reducer fork-resolution is triggered during event replay, which is a core CRDT operation that may be re-executed multiple times (e.g., full replay from scratch, sync from a peer). The plan does not specify that `alert_store.append` is idempotent or deduplicated, so each full replay will append a duplicate marker for the same fork.
findings.location: What/files — item 2: reducer/_processors.py ~L159-167, alert_store.append
findings.suggested_fix: Key each marker by `(ticket_id, dropped_event_uuid)` so that `alert_store.append` (or equivalent) can be made a set-insert / upsert rather than a plain append, ensuring idempotency across replays.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen AC item 3 (and the test for item 4) to assert the exit-10 behavior of `claim_compute` directly — e.g., require that running `claim` on the losing clone (after merge is visible) returns exit code 10, not merely that a marker exists — eliminating the 'or' disjunction so the claim.py code path is mandated by the test.
findings.finding: The regression test's Acceptance Criterion for item (b) uses a disjunction — 'exit-10 path **or** the surfaced marker' — which allows the test to satisfy the AC solely by checking the durable marker, without ever invoking or asserting the post-claim verification code path added to `claim.py` (item 1). This means item 1 could be unimplemented or silently broken while `make test` stays green.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, bullet 1 and 3; 'What / files' item 1 vs. item 4 test assertion (b)
findings.suggested_fix: In the regression test, after both clones push and merge, re-run (or simulate) the post-claim verification step on the losing clone and assert it receives exit code 10. Keep the marker/fsck assertion as a separate, additional assertion rather than an alternative. Update AC item 1 and 3 to require BOTH signals (exit-10 AND durable marker) to be present and tested.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit step to What/files for updating `docs/concurrency.md` (or demote AC4 to a note if it is out-of-scope), so every AC maps to at least one described implementation step.
findings.finding: AC4 ('docs/concurrency.md notes the surfacing behaviour') has no corresponding implementation step in the What/files section or the Scope 'In' list, leaving it unreachable by the described work.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4 ('docs/concurrency.md notes the surfacing behaviour')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a step 5 to What/files: '**Documentation** — `docs/concurrency.md`: add a subsection describing (a) how a cross-clone claim race is surfaced to the loser (exit-10 path), (b) the durable alert marker location, and (c) how `rebar fsck`/`show` surfaces the resolved fork.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a What/files item for `docs/concurrency.md` describing the content to be added (e.g., 'document that a losing clone receives exit-10 post-merge, that fork resolution is surfaced as a fsck finding, and how to interpret the bridge_alerts marker').
findings.finding: AC item 4 ('docs/concurrency.md notes the surfacing behaviour') is an orphan criterion — no corresponding deliverable or file-level work item appears anywhere in the What/files decomposition.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria item 4 vs. What/files items 1-4
findings.suggested_fix: Add as item 5 in What/files: '**docs/concurrency.md** — add a subsection describing: (a) cross-clone claim races and the lower-UUID-wins STATUS resolution rule, (b) how losing clones detect the loss (exit-10 / ConcurrencyMismatch), (c) the durable bridge_alerts marker and how to inspect it via fsck/show.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in Item 1 whether the post-claim re-read must be preceded by a fetch from origin (to receive the competing clone's events), and define the fallback behaviour when the fetch or push is unavailable (offline/no-remote).
findings.finding: The post-claim verification protocol (Item 1) does not specify whether a fetch from origin is required before re-reading the merged store, leaving the detection window undefined.
findings.location: What/files §1 (claim.py ~L170-175, after push.push_after_commit); Acceptance Criteria line 1

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief positive rationale for each mechanism choice: (1) why the fork-loss signal fires at the specific timing proposed (post-push vs. post-sync), and (2) why alert_store.append was chosen over a BRIDGE_ALERT event for the durable marker.
findings.finding: The plan states no positive rationale for either (a) the post-claim re-read timing choice (why immediately after push vs. at read-sync time) or (b) the alert_store.append mechanism choice (why reconciler-tier storage vs. a BRIDGE_ALERT event). Absence of rationale for both load-bearing design decisions leaves the implementer unable to evaluate whether the approach is correct or whether a discovered constraint (e.g., push fails, or no Jira integration) requires a design change.
findings.location: What / files — items 1 and 2 (mechanism rationale absent from both)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the OR in AC1: state explicitly that BOTH the exit-10 retroactive check (step 1) AND the durable alert marker (step 2) are required, and update AC1 to reflect both deliverables.
findings.finding: AC1 uses 'a retroactive exit-10 path, OR an equivalent explicit signal' while What/files step 1 (exit-10) AND step 2 (durable marker) are both described as required — the OR makes minimum passing criteria ambiguous.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1; What/files steps 1 and 2
findings.suggested_fix: Rewrite AC1 as: 'A claim that loses the cross-clone race (a) returns exit code 10 with a clear message when the claim path detects the loss post-merge, AND (b) records a durable alert marker — both covered by the two-clone regression test.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the `show` read path file(s) (`reads_cli.py` and/or `_lib_reads.py`/`_reads.py`) to §What/files §3 and to the Scope 'In' list, specifying where the fork-marker annotation is injected into the show output.
findings.finding: The 'show' annotation (AC2 / §3) requires changes to the `show` read surfaces (`reads_cli.py`, `_lib_reads.py`, `_reads.py`, and/or `_mcp_reads.py`), none of which are named in §What/files or in the Scope 'In' list. These are the only code paths that render a `show` response to callers.
findings.location: What/files §3 — 'fsck/validate/show' / Scope 'In'

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify error-handling for the `alert_store.append` call in the reducer fork path: at minimum, log a warning and continue (degraded mode) so a marker-write failure never aborts a valid merge, and the failure itself is observable.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify error handling for the `alert_store.append` call inside the reducer's fork-resolution path. If the write fails (disk full, permission error, etc.), it is unclear whether the error is surfaced, swallowed silently, or allowed to propagate and abort an otherwise-successful merge.
findings.location: What/files — item 2: reducer/_processors.py ~L159-167
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the `alert_store.append` call in a try/except, log the failure at WARNING level (e.g. 'FORK_MARKER_WRITE_FAILED: {ticket} — {err}'), and allow the fork resolution to succeed regardless. Add a note to the plan that marker writes are best-effort.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command to AC2, e.g. `rebar fsck | grep -E 'STATUS_FORK_RESOLVED:'` (or the equivalent test assertion), and disambiguate 'and/or' — state whether both fsck and show surfacing are required or just one.
findings.finding: AC2 uses 'and/or' ('fsck (or validate) finding and/or a show annotation') without specifying a concrete proving command or expected output format, leaving verification of this criterion under-specified.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2
findings.suggested_fix: Rewrite AC2 as: 'A resolved STATUS fork appears as a `STATUS_FORK_RESOLVED` fsck finding (verified by `rebar fsck` output containing the tag) AND as a show annotation on the affected ticket; the regression test asserts both.' Drop the and/or.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add a concrete work item for `_commands/txn.py` to the What/files decomposition (describing what changes are needed there), or remove it from the Scope 'In' list if it requires no changes.
findings.finding: `_commands/txn.py` is listed in the Scope section as in-scope but has no corresponding work item or file-level change described anywhere in the What/files decomposition, creating a dangling scope entry that will confuse implementers.
findings.location: Scope section ('In: claim path (_commands/claim.py, _commands/txn.py)') vs. What/files items 1-4
findings.suggested_fix: If the claim-lost retroactive signal needs a transaction-layer hook in txn.py, add a What/files item: '_commands/txn.py: expose a helper that re-reads the merged ticket state so claim_compute can call it post-push'. Otherwise, remove txn.py from the Scope line.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a RETIRE/cleanup path for `bridge_alerts` fork markers (e.g., prune when the ticket reaches a terminal state, or cap per-ticket, or note explicitly that markers are intentionally permanent and bounded by ticket count).
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new durable-marker write (`alert_store.append` in `bridge_alerts`) during reducer fork resolution, but specifies no retirement or cleanup path. Every fork-resolution event writes a persistent marker that accumulates indefinitely, with no defined point at which stale markers are pruned.
findings.location: What/files — item 2: 'record a durable marker (reuse alert_store.append, bridge_alerts dir)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC or Scope note: markers are retired when the ticket is closed/archived (or: one marker per ticket, overwritten on repeat forks). If permanent accumulation is acceptable, document it explicitly and note the fsck reader must handle large marker sets gracefully.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] (Advisory) Consider whether the immediate post-claim exit-10 path (item 1) and the durable-marker + fsck/show surfacing (items 2–3) are best delivered as one unit or as a thin first slice (exit-10) followed by a second slice (marker + fsck) — each is independently valuable and testable.
findings.finding: The plan bundles two independently-valuable surfacing mechanisms — (1) the immediate post-claim exit-10 signal for the losing claimant and (2) the durable marker + fsck/show annotation path for later diagnostic discovery — that serve slightly different actor personas (the losing agent at claim-time vs. a later operator running fsck/show) and touch entirely different subsystems (claim.py vs. reducer/_processors.py vs. fsck.py). Each piece would deliver standalone value: exit-10 alone addresses the 'agent proceeds unaware' problem; the fsck/marker path alone addresses the 'silent fork in history' observability problem. This is an advisory observation, not a blocking concern, since the test couples them and they share the same root audit finding.
findings.location: ## What / files — items 1–3 and ## Acceptance Criteria — AC lines 1–2

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add a concrete work item for `_commands/txn.py` to §What/files (specifying the exact change needed), or remove it from the Scope 'In' list if no change is actually required.
findings.finding: `_commands/txn.py` is listed in Scope 'In' but has no corresponding work item or file-level change described in §What/files; it is an unenumerated edit target with no specified change.
findings.location: Scope 'In' — 'claim path (_commands/claim.py, _commands/txn.py)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether `alert_store.append` for STATUS fork markers is replay-safe/idempotent (deduplicated by ticket+winner UUID), and specify whether a one-time replay of existing stores is needed to backfill markers for historical forks — or explicitly scope AC2 to future-only forks.
findings.finding: The plan introduces durable `alert_store.append` markers for STATUS fork resolutions going forward, but specifies no backfill for historical forks that already occurred before this code ships. Existing stores that experienced cross-clone claim races will have no markers, so fsck and `show` annotations will silently miss all pre-implementation forks — contradicting AC2 ('a resolved STATUS fork appears as an fsck finding') if read as applying to existing stores.
findings.location: What/files §2 (reducer/_processors.py ~L159-167) and AC2 ('A resolved STATUS fork appears as an fsck (or validate) finding and/or a show annotation')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a dedup guard (e.g. check `alert_store.is_deduped(f'fork:{ticket_id}:{winner_uuid}', ...)` before appending) so re-replaying a forked ticket does not produce duplicate markers, and note in the plan whether existing stores require a one-time `rebar validate --repair` pass to populate historical markers.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
